name: AlgoBlock
short_description: A set of physically connectable command blocks that children assemble by hand to build Logo-like programs, designed to support collaborative learning.
long_description: AlgoBlock, presented by Hideyuki Suzuki and Hiroshi Kato at the first International Conference on Computer Support for Collaborative Learning (CSCL 1995), is a set of graspable physical blocks that each correspond to a command in a Logo-like language; learners snap the blocks together on a shared work surface to construct a program that drives an on-screen submarine. By making the program a manipulable physical artifact placed in shared space, it deliberately turns coding into a visible, jointly accessible activity that stimulates conversation and cooperation among learners. It is widely credited as an early instance of tangible programming and a foundational example of tangible user interfaces, where computation is represented and manipulated through physical objects rather than only screen symbols.
ai_note: AlgoBlock is a tangible programming interface where physical blocks map to fixed Logo-like commands; there is no machine learning or AI inference in the system.

images.caption: Learners collaboratively assembling AlgoBlock's connectable command blocks to build a program.
images.alt_text: Photograph of people gathered around a table connecting rectangular physical command blocks together to form a tangible computer program.

sources.title: Interaction-level support for collaborative learning: AlgoBlock — an open programming language
